title: Younger Supplementary Legends from MS Bodley 779 (Horstmann selection)
summary: CME00077 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding that joins Carl Horstmann's two 1889 Archiv installments, printed on pp. 307–353 and 369–422. Horstmann selected thirty-three anonymous Middle English verse legends from Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 779 rather than printing that manuscript's complete South English Legendary compilation. Bodley 779 is the edition's direct source and Horstmann's claimed sole witness for the group or versions; DIMEV 1.0 nevertheless indexes a parallel Feast of Corpus Christi witness in British Library Egerton MS 2810, ff. 176v–179v. A complete 532-page journal volume, the pinned official Bodley catalog TEI, the complete DIMEV 1.0 data release, Kanno's complete 2016 thesis, and Digital Bodleian's complete 672-canvas manuscript project are cached locally. The full-codex image bundle retains boards, endleaves, every published manuscript leaf, parchment strengtheners, and detached medieval-binding leather. Its work_portion maps all thirty-three selected legends separately, including shared writing zones: Remigius begins partway down fol. 229av beside Barlaam and Josaphat, and Germanus ends partway down fol. 241av where Aethelwold begins. Egerton 2810 has no public complete facsimile in the current British Library record or October 2025 digitised-manuscript list; the manifest records a whole-object Imaging Services order route.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00077
entities.description: A public transcription that joins two non-contiguous journal installments and preserves thirty-three legend divisions, printed page breaks, manuscript folio milestones, editorial notes, and italicized abbreviation expansions or supplied letters.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 779
entities.description: A paper codex of the second quarter of the fifteenth century containing an idiosyncratic South English Legendary compilation. The selected legends occur discontinuously from fol. 172r through the Germanus text ending in the current digital fol. 241av; numerous excluded legends intervene, and later selected lives share surfaces with Barlaam and Josaphat.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Second quarter of the fifteenth century

physical_description.extent: ii + 312 leaves; foliation 1–306 omits inserted leaves now lettered after 110, 128, 141, 229, 241, and 252; former-binding matter and recovered fragments are stored with the manuscript
physical_description.format: Paper codex with a one-column layout, 41–60 written lines
notes: Written and extensively corrected, likely by one scribe in multiple phases, in cursive Anglicana.

The contemporary English white-leather-on-boards binding was replaced in 1998; its two wooden boards, thongs, straps, leather, parchment strengtheners, and recovered fragments are preserved in a phase box and fascicule.
Presented in 1611 by William Harwood, prebendary of Winchester.
Görlach states that the compiler modifies source-text orthography toward a Hampshire dialect; this is not evidence that every legend was composed there.

entities.label: British Library Egerton MS 2810
entities.description: A fourteenth-century parchment South English Legendary manuscript with fifteenth-century additions. Its Feast of Corpus Christi at ff. 176v–179v is the only parallel witness indexed by DIMEV for any of the thirty-three CME00077 parts; Horstmann did not use it for the 1889 edition.

physical_description.format: Codex of English verse saints' lives
notes: The British Library catalog reports a large lacuna between ff. 87v and 88 and several fifteenth-century additions in different hands.
The Feast of Corpus Christi begins at fol. 176v; DIMEV gives its end as fol. 179v.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Archiv volume 82 (1889)
entities.description: A complete 532-page journal-volume PDF. The two encoded installments appear at PDF pages 315–361 and 377–430; the intervening PDF pages 362–376 preserve the unrelated printed pp. 354–368 omitted by CME00077.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete project facsimile of MS. Bodl. 779
entities.description: The provider's complete 672-canvas published project inventory, including boards, endleaves, every published manuscript leaf, parchment strengtheners, and detached original-binding leather. A practical 1600-pixel derivative of every canvas is cached without cropping to the work.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest / 672 color JPEG access derivatives

entities.label: Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts record for MS. Bodl. 779
entities.description: Andrew Dunning's repository record, based partly on DIMEV and Manfred Görlach and revised through consultation of the original, supplies detailed contents, exact loci, date, codicology, dialect note, provenance, and full-facsimile link.

entities.label: DIMEV 1.0 records for the thirty-three selected legends
entities.description: Versioned scholarly index records treating the selected texts individually and documenting their incipits, witnesses, loci, and edition references.

entities.label: Mami Kanno, study of Bodley 779 as a hagiographic compilation (2016)
entities.description: A doctoral thesis supporting manuscript-level treatment of the plural, fluid South English Legendaries and describing Bodley 779's date, North Hampshire language, unique saints, compilation, and groups of papal and native saints' lives.

entities.label: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record for Egerton MS 2810
entities.description: The current official record describes the South English Legendary contents, identifies the Feast of Corpus Christi at fol. 176v, and provides the physical-access and reference-enquiry routes; it exposes no digitised-content link.

access.access_method: Open the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts record.

No public complete page-image object was found. The exact catalog has no Digitised Content link, and the 10 October 2025 complete digitised-manuscript list passes directly from Egerton MS 2809 to Egerton MS 2818 without listing 2810.
notes: This negative finding is scoped to a complete public page-image facsimile; the physical manuscript remains extant and requestable.

access.access_method: Use the British Library online Imaging Services order form. Request the whole current manuscript—covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every recto and verso through fol. 188, edges and any associated fragments where offered—and specify colour photography. Identify the Corpus Christi work portion separately as ff. 176v–179v.
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services; licensing@bl.uk for reuse questions
access.format: Digital reference or reproduction photography; exact availability, handling, format, and whole-object quote subject to repository review
notes: The published 2025/26 rate card lists photographic TIFF at £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 per subsequent single-page image, while complex handling is £58.19 per image; a complete 188-folio manuscript request is a large order and should be treated as a custom quote rather than estimated mechanically.
The service says original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned and that colour must be requested explicitly where a surrogate exists.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval texts and physical witness substantially predate modern copyright.

rights.component: Fourteenth-century Feast of Corpus Christi witness
rights.basis: The anonymous medieval text and physical witness substantially predate modern copyright.

British Library Imaging Services says supplied material may carry rights and must not be copied or reproduced without written permission; order-specific terms would govern any delivered whole-manuscript images.
notes: A paid research delivery must not be placed in the public repository unless the order terms and a written licence expressly permit that reuse.

rights.basis: The exact U-M item states that the materials are in the public domain and applies the No Copyright – United States rights statement.
The exact IIIF manifest attributes the photographs to Bodleian Libraries and applies CC BY-NC 4.0.
notes: Redistribution is allowed only for non-commercial purposes with attribution and compliance with Digital Bodleian terms; commercial print-on-demand use requires separate permission.

rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data and repository contents

notes: The unaltered thesis may be redistributed noncommercially with attribution under CC BY-NC-ND 4.0; commercial or derivative reuse requires separate permission.

rights.basis: The underlying 1889 edition is public domain, but no separate license for the exact scan file was relied upon.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A large manuscript-specific South English Legendary compilation
editorial_practices.representation: Thirty-three selected supplementary legends across two installments
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann selected particular younger additions or versions and did not print the manuscript continuously; CME joins only those two printed runs.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Italic letters and strings in Horstmann's transcription
editorial_practices.description: The encoding preserves Horstmann's italics, used extensively for abbreviation expansions or editorially supplied letters; exact semantics must be checked against the manuscript and edition at each passage.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript foliation milestones embedded in Horstmann's edition
editorial_practices.description: Folio milestones are sparse and include at least one apparent typo: Soter has milestone 182 where the manuscript sequence and current catalog support fol. 232.

evidence.summary: Confirms repository identity, structure, selected texts, page breaks, folio milestones, notes, and encoding practices.

evidence.summary: Confirms shelfmark, date, English origin, dialect note, complete contents and target loci, DIMEV references, physical description, provenance, binding fragments, and full Digital Bodleian surrogate.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF manifest and complete local bundle for MS. Bodl. 779, audited 2026-07-12.
evidence.locator: All 672 canvases inventoried; selected runs mapped across one-based bundle inventory positions 357–498
evidence.summary: ZIP integrity and inventory audit confirm complete provider-project coverage. Visual checks confirm Corpus Christi at fol. 172r, Remigius beginning in the lower part of fol. 229av beside Barlaam and Josaphat, Germanus beginning at fol. 240v and ending above Aethelwold on provider-labeled fol. 241av, and inclusion of detached binding matter.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian exact IIIF attribution and Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: The exact manifest says Photo © Bodleian Libraries, University of Oxford and applies CC BY-NC 4.0; terms require attribution, restrict commercial use, and provide an imaging/licensing enquiry route.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0 source-data release.
evidence.summary: Versioned scholarly index data supporting individual text identities, incipits, witness loci, and editions. Record 5329 has two witnesses—Bodley 779 and Egerton 2810—while each of the other thirty-two cited records has one indexed witness in this release; the bundled README and LICENSE apply CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: Mami Kanno, 'A Hagiographic Compilation of Medieval Native Women in the South English Legendaries: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Bodley 779' (PhD thesis, King's College London, 2016).
evidence.locator: front matter and repository EULA; pp. 21–26, 270
evidence.summary: Supports treating the South English Legendaries as plural, fluid manuscript collections and describes Bodley 779's date, North Hampshire language, idiosyncratic compilation, unique saints, and papal-life grouping. The front matter applies CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 and permits unaltered noncommercial distribution with attribution.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Egerton MS 2810, record 032-001984866.
evidence.locator: Item 69, Feast of Corpus Christi, fol. 176v; physical description and access fields
evidence.summary: Confirms the current shelfmark, parchment support, 188-folio extent, fourteenth-century main manuscript with fifteenth-century additions, the Feast of Corpus Christi opening at fol. 176v, custodial history, physical-access route, and absence of an exposed Digitised Content link.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitised manuscripts and archives shelfmark list, updated 10 October 2025.
evidence.locator: PDF p. 72: Egerton sequence lists MS 2809 and then MS 2818, with no MS 2810
evidence.summary: The complete 261-page provider list of then-available digitised manuscripts does not include Egerton MS 2810, corroborating the current catalog's blank Digitised Content field.
notes: The PDF opens and text-extracts completely. qpdf reports inconsistent linearization hint-table offsets but no failed page access; the exact provider bytes and checksum are preserved.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation Services and online ordering.
evidence.summary: Provides the current public order form and required shelfmark, title, folio, format, colour, payment, and delivery information; says original manuscripts receive photography rather than scanning and large or special orders are subject to review.

evidence.citation: British Library Digitisation and Reproduction Services price list, 2025/26 rates.
evidence.locator: p. 1, photographic TIFF and complex-handling rates
evidence.summary: Lists £27.70 for a first photographic TIFF image, £7.25 for subsequent single-page images from the same item, and £58.19 per complex-handling image; projects above £10,000 require contact.
notes: The PDF opens and text-extracts completely. qpdf reports non-fatal linearization hint warnings; the exact provider bytes and checksum are preserved.

evidence.citation: British Library Imaging Services terms and conditions, updated January 2026.
evidence.summary: States turnaround and large-order conditions, makes customers responsible for third-party rights, and prohibits copying or reproducing supplied material without written permission.

open_questions.question: For which of the thirty-three texts does current scholarship still support Horstmann's 1889 claim that Bodley 779 is the only manuscript?
next_steps: Review the witness census in every DIMEV record and its cited current bibliography.
notes: DIMEV 1.0 contradicts a literal sole-witness reading for Corpus Christi by listing Egerton 2810, ff. 176v–179v; its other thirty-two cited records each contain one indexed witness. The remaining question concerns updates in post-release scholarship.

open_questions.question: Can the Bodleian provide a formal concordance between the catalog's Germanus locus fols. 240v–241v and current Digital Bodleian labels fol. 240v through fol. 241av?
next_steps: Ask the Bodleian cataloging team only if a formal foliation crosswalk becomes necessary; retain both current claims meanwhile.

review_notes: The two printed runs are modeled as separate edition installments because the volume contents lists them separately and unrelated matter intervenes.
Whole-source completeness and work coverage are kept separate: the manuscript cache is the complete 672-canvas provider project, while work_portion records thirty-three discontiguous selections.
The broad facsimile start/end links are navigation anchors only; every included legend retains its own folio and canvas range.
The official catalog's date and Hampshire dialect statement describe the physical compilation and its scribe-compiler, not a single composition event for all selected legends.

The public manuscript photographs are CC BY-NC 4.0 and cannot be used in the repository's commercial print-on-demand pathway without separate permission, even though the medieval texts themselves are public domain.

DIMEV 1.0 exposes one parallel witness outside Bodley 779: Corpus Christi also survives in Egerton 2810. The current British Library catalog exposes no images, and the complete October 2025 digitised list omits that shelfmark, so the whole-object route requests all of Egerton 2810 and maps ff. 176v–179v separately.
